subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Takeshi slips and falls at a grocery store on April 15, 2024 and sustains personal injuries. He decides to consult a lawyer on November 1, 2025.
call_of_question: What is the last day Takeshi may commence a proceeding for his personal injury claim, assuming he discovered his claim on the date of the fall?

options.D: There is no limitation period for personal injury claims in Ontario

explanation: Under s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002, a proceeding must be commenced within two years of the day the claim was discovered. Takeshi discovered his claim on April 15, 2024 (the date of the fall), so the limitation period expires on April 15, 2026. His decision to consult a lawyer on November 1, 2025 does not affect the calculation.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. April 15, 2026 is exactly two years after the date of discovery (April 15, 2024) under s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002.
why_B_wrong: November 1, 2027 is two years after the lawyer consultation. The limitation period runs from the date of discovery, not from the date of legal consultation.
why_C_wrong: April 15, 2025 would be only one year after the fall. The basic limitation period is two years, not one year.
why_D_wrong: The general two-year limitation period in s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002 applies to personal injury claims. There is no blanket exception for personal injury.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Sylviane operates a catering business. Her supplier breached a contract on June 1, 2023, and she discovered the breach on the same day. She did not commence an action. In January 2025, her accountant told her she should sue to recover her losses. Sylviane commences a breach of contract action on June 15, 2025.
call_of_question: Which statement is correct regarding Sylviane's action?

options.A: The action is not statute-barred because the accountant's advice in January 2025 restarted the limitation period.
options.B: The action is statute-barred because more than two years passed since the claim was discovered on June 1, 2023.
options.C: A six-year limitation period applies to breach of contract claims in Ontario.
options.D: The limitation period was suspended while Sylviane sought professional advice about whether to sue.

explanation: Sylviane discovered her breach of contract claim on June 1, 2023. Under s. 4 of the Limitations Act, 2002, she had two years — until June 1, 2025 — to commence proceedings. Commencing on June 15, 2025 is 14 days after the expiry of the limitation period. The action is therefore statute-barred. An accountant's advice does not restart or suspend the limitation period.
why_A_wrong: Professional advice does not restart, toll, or otherwise affect the running of the limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The claim was discovered on June 1, 2023; the two-year period expired June 1, 2025; commencement on June 15, 2025 is out of time.
why_C_wrong: The Limitations Act, 2002 abolished the prior six-year limitation period for most contract claims. The general two-year period now applies.
why_D_wrong: There is no provision in the Limitations Act, 2002 that suspends the limitation period while a party seeks accounting or professional advice.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4

tested_concepts: professional advice does not toll limitation

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Priya was sexually assaulted by her employer in 2015. She did not report the assault at the time. In 2024, following therapy, she comes to understand the full psychological harm caused to her and wishes to bring a civil claim.
call_of_question: Which statement best describes the applicable limitation period for Priya's claim?

options.A: The claim is statute-barred because more than two years have passed since the assault occurred in 2015.
options.B: No limitation period applies to Priya's claim because sexual assault falls within s. 16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002.
options.C: The discoverability principle defers the start of the two-year period to 2024, so Priya has until 2026 to commence her action.
options.D: A 15-year ultimate limitation period applies to Priya's claim, which will expire in 2030.

explanation: Section 16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002 provides that there is no limitation period with respect to a claim based on a sexual assault. This removes the limitation period entirely — not merely defers it via discoverability. Furthermore, s. 16(1)(h) also excludes such claims from the 15-year ultimate limitation period in s. 15 of the Act. Priya may commence her claim at any time.
why_A_wrong: Section 16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002 creates an exception for sexual assault claims. The two-year period in s. 4 does not apply.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 16(1)(h) provides that no limitation period applies to a claim based on sexual assault.
why_C_wrong: While the discoverability principle in s. 5 could theoretically defer the start of the limitation period, s. 16(1)(h) goes further — it eliminates the limitation period entirely for sexual assault claims.
why_D_wrong: The 15-year ultimate limitation period in s. 15 does not apply to claims exempted under s. 16, which includes sexual assault claims under s. 16(1)(h).

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Claims with No Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 16(1)(h)

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Reuben is owed $10,000 under a contract made in 2000. He never commenced an action to recover the debt. It is now 2026. Reuben argues that the Limitations Act, 2002 does not apply to his claim because the contract was made before the Act came into force on January 1, 2004.
call_of_question: Which is the most accurate statement regarding Reuben's claim?

options.A: The former Limitations Act (with its six-year limitation period) applies to contracts made before January 1, 2004, so Reuben may still have time to sue.
options.B: The Limitations Act, 2002 does not apply retroactively to claims that arose before January 1, 2004.
options.C: Reuben's claim is statute-barred; the Limitations Act, 2002 and its transitional rules extinguished pre-existing claims where the applicable limitation period had already expired.
options.D: Reuben can rely on an acknowledgment of the debt made by the debtor at any time to restart the limitation period, even now.

explanation: The Limitations Act, 2002 contains transitional provisions that address pre-existing claims. Even applying the former six-year limitation period to a 2000 contract claim, that period would have expired by 2006 at the latest. By 2026, Reuben's claim is long extinguished. Moreover, once a limitation period has expired and a claim is statute-barred, a subsequent acknowledgment of the debt cannot revive it — acknowledgment must occur within the running limitation period to restart the clock.
why_A_wrong: Even under the former six-year limitation period, a contract claim from 2000 would have expired by 2006. Reuben's claim is extinguished regardless of which Act applies.
why_B_wrong: The Limitations Act, 2002 does contain transitional provisions that apply to pre-existing claims. The claim is not preserved simply because it arose before 2004.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under either the former or current limitations regime, Reuben's claim is long statute-barred and extinguished.
why_D_wrong: Acknowledgment of a debt can restart a limitation period only if it is made while the limitation period is still running. Once a claim is extinguished, acknowledgment cannot revive it.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 4; transitional provisions

tested_concepts: acknowledgment must occur during running period

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Farah loaned $25,000 to her sister Leila on January 15, 2020, repayable on demand. Farah made a written demand for repayment on March 1, 2023. Leila did not repay the loan.
call_of_question: What is the last day Farah may commence a court proceeding to recover the loan?

options.C: There is no limitation period because this is a family loan.

explanation: For demand obligations, the discoverability provisions of s. 5 of the Limitations Act, 2002 operate so that the limitation period begins to run when demand is made (and not paid), because it is only at that point that the claimant knows a proceeding would be an appropriate means to seek to remedy the failure. Farah made demand on March 1, 2023; two years from that date is March 1, 2025.
why_A_wrong: January 15, 2022 is two years after the loan was made. For demand loans, the limitation period does not run from the date of the loan but from the date demand is made.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The two-year limitation period for a demand obligation begins when demand is made and refused. Demand was made March 1, 2023, so the period expires March 1, 2025.
why_C_wrong: The Limitations Act, 2002 contains no exception for family loans. The general two-year limitation period applies.
why_D_wrong: January 15, 2025 is two years from the loan date, not from the demand date. For demand obligations, the clock runs from the date of demand.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: The Basic Two-Year Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 4, 5

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discoverability — When a Claim is Discovered
fact_pattern: Marco fell from scaffolding at a construction site on May 1, 2022 and was hospitalized with serious injuries. He knew immediately that the contractor had erected the scaffolding and that the fall had caused his injuries. He was not aware until January 10, 2024 that the contractor had violated OSHA regulations.
call_of_question: On what date was Marco's claim against the contractor 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?

options.C: The date his lawyer first advised him to sue
options.D: The date an OSHA investigation report was issued

explanation: Under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim is discovered on the day the person first knew or ought to have known: (a) the injury occurred; (b) the injury was caused by the act or omission of the defendant; (c) the identity of the defendant; and (d) that a proceeding would be an appropriate means to remedy the harm. Marco knew all four elements on May 1, 2022: he was injured, the contractor caused his injury, he knew the contractor's identity, and a proceeding was appropriate. Knowledge of a specific OSHA violation is not a required element of discovery.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. All four elements of s. 5(1) were satisfied on May 1, 2022 — Marco knew the injury, the cause, the defendant's identity, and that a proceeding was appropriate.
why_B_wrong: Knowledge of OSHA regulatory violations is not a required element of s. 5(1) discovery. Marco knew enough on May 1, 2022 to satisfy all four branches.
why_C_wrong: Legal advice is not a prescribed element of s. 5(1) discovery. The date a lawyer advises a client to sue does not determine when the claim was discovered.
why_D_wrong: An OSHA investigation report is not required under s. 5(1). Marco already had knowledge of all relevant elements on the date of the fall.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discoverability — Section 5
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)

tested_concepts: OSHA violation not a discovery element

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discoverability — When a Claim is Discovered
fact_pattern: Nadia underwent abdominal surgery in February 2020. She recovered and had no reason to suspect anything was wrong. In March 2023, she began experiencing severe pain. A specialist informed her in March 2023 that a surgical sponge had been left inside her during the 2020 surgery and was the cause of her pain. She had no reason to suspect a problem before March 2023.
call_of_question: When was Nadia's medical malpractice claim 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?

options.A: February 2020, when the surgery was performed and the sponge was left inside her
options.B: March 2023, when she first knew that her injury was caused by the surgeon's act or omission
options.C: The date the surgical sponge was inserted, because the act occurred then
options.D: Six years after the surgery, under the former limitation period for medical malpractice

explanation: Under s. 5(1)(b) of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim is not discovered until the claimant knew or ought to have known that the injury was caused by an act or omission of the defendant. Nadia had no symptoms and no reason to investigate until March 2023. The s. 5(2) presumption (that the claim was discovered on the day of the act) is rebutted because she did not know and could not reasonably have known before March 2023. Discovery therefore occurred in March 2023.
why_A_wrong: The s. 5(2) presumption that the claim was discovered on the date of the act is rebutted here. Nadia had no symptoms and no reason to suspect a problem in February 2020.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Nadia first knew the cause of her injury — the retained sponge — in March 2023, satisfying s. 5(1)(b).
why_C_wrong: The date of the act and the date of discovery are the same when the claimant knows of the act. Here, Nadia did not know the sponge was left inside her until March 2023.
why_D_wrong: The former six-year limitation period for most claims was abolished by the Limitations Act, 2002. The general two-year period applies, running from the date of discovery.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discoverability — Section 5
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 5(1), 5(2)

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discoverability — When a Claim is Discovered
fact_pattern: Obiageli was injured in a hit-and-run car accident in 2021. She knew immediately that she was injured and that a vehicle had struck her, but the driver fled the scene. Obiageli spent approximately eight months working with police and her insurer trying to identify the driver before she gave up. She finally identified the driver through a witness in July 2023.
call_of_question: When did Obiageli's claim against the driver likely become 'discovered' under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002?

options.A: The day of the accident in 2021, because she knew she was injured and that a vehicle caused the injury
options.B: Eight months after the accident, when she stopped investigating, because she ought to have discovered the defendant's identity by then
options.C: July 2023, when she actually identified the driver, because s. 5(1)(c) requires knowledge of the identity of the defendant
options.D: She has no viable claim because she failed to sue within two years of the accident

explanation: Section 5(1)(c) of the Limitations Act, 2002 provides that a claim is not discovered until the claimant knows or ought to know the identity of the defendant. Obiageli could not have commenced proceedings against an unknown defendant. She diligently investigated and identified the driver in July 2023. Under s. 5(1)(c), discovery occurred when she identified the driver. She then has two years from July 2023 to commence her action.
why_A_wrong: Knowing that injury occurred and that a vehicle caused it does not satisfy s. 5(1)(c), which requires knowledge of the defendant's identity. Obiageli did not know who the driver was in 2021.
why_B_wrong: Simply stopping an investigation does not mean a claimant 'ought to have known' the defendant's identity if reasonable investigation did not reveal it. The objective standard considers what a reasonable person could discover with diligent effort.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 5(1)(c) requires knowledge of the defendant's identity, which was not obtained until July 2023.
why_D_wrong: Section 5(1)(c) postpones discovery — and therefore the start of the two-year period — until the defendant's identity is known or ought to be known. Obiageli's claim is not necessarily extinguished.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discoverability — Section 5
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)(c)

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discoverability — When a Claim is Discovered
fact_pattern: Gideon believed he was defrauded by his investment advisor in 2020 when his portfolio lost value through suspicious trades. He consulted a lawyer in 2020 who told him he had no case. Gideon did not investigate further. In 2024, a second lawyer reviewed the same facts and told Gideon he clearly had a strong fraud claim.
call_of_question: Which statement best describes when Gideon's claim against the investment advisor was 'discovered' for the purposes of the Limitations Act, 2002?

options.A: 2024, when the second lawyer confirmed he had a viable claim
options.B: Likely 2020, because the 'ought to know' standard in s. 5(1) is objective and a reasonable person with Gideon's knowledge would have known enough to commence proceedings, despite the first lawyer's incorrect advice
options.C: The date the first lawyer gave incorrect advice, which paused the running of the limitation period
options.D: 2026, because reliance on a lawyer's advice always suspends the limitation period

explanation: The discoverability standard in s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002 includes both a subjective and objective element: the claimant must know or ought to know the relevant facts. The 'ought to know' test is objective — it asks what a reasonable person with the claimant's abilities and circumstances would have known. Gideon was aware of the suspicious trades in 2020. A reasonable person in his position would have known enough to recognize that a proceeding was appropriate. Incorrect legal advice does not toll or suspend the limitation period. Discovery likely occurred in 2020, and the two-year period would have expired in 2022.
why_A_wrong: Subjective confirmation by a lawyer that a claim exists is not the test. The objective 'ought to know' standard applies, and Gideon likely knew enough in 2020.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The objective standard in s. 5(1) applies regardless of incorrect legal advice; discovery likely occurred in 2020.
why_C_wrong: Incorrect legal advice does not pause, toll, or suspend the running of a limitation period under the Limitations Act, 2002.
why_D_wrong: There is no provision in the Limitations Act, 2002 that suspends the limitation period based on reliance on legal advice, whether correct or incorrect.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discoverability — Section 5
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(1)

tested_concepts: incorrect legal advice does not toll limitation

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Discoverability — When a Claim is Discovered
fact_pattern: A defendant in a civil action argues that the plaintiff's claim is statute-barred because the alleged act or omission occurred more than two years ago. The plaintiff argues that she did not discover her claim until more recently.
call_of_question: Who bears the burden of proving that the claim was discovered on a date later than the date the act or omission occurred?

options.A: The defendant must prove the claim was discovered on or before the date of the act or omission
options.B: Neither party; the court determines the date of discovery independently based on the evidence
options.C: The plaintiff, who must rebut the presumption in s. 5(2) of the Limitations Act, 2002 that the claim was discovered on the day the act or omission occurred
options.D: The burden shifts between the parties depending on which party has better access to the relevant evidence

explanation: Section 5(2) of the Limitations Act, 2002 creates a presumption that a claim is presumed to have been discovered on the day the act or omission occurred. A plaintiff who wishes to rely on a later discovery date must rebut this presumption. The burden therefore rests on the plaintiff to establish that she did not know and could not reasonably have known the relevant facts on the earlier date.
why_A_wrong: Section 5(2) creates a presumption favouring the defendant (i.e., that discovery occurred on the date of the act). The plaintiff must rebut it, not the defendant.
why_B_wrong: Courts do not determine discovery independently without regard to who bears the burden. Section 5(2) expressly places the onus on the plaintiff to rebut the presumption.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The plaintiff bears the burden of rebutting the s. 5(2) presumption to establish a later discovery date.
why_D_wrong: There is no shifting burden based on access to evidence in the Limitations Act, 2002. The statutory presumption places the burden squarely on the plaintiff.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Discoverability — Section 5
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5(2)

tested_concepts: date of discovery vs. act or omission

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Miriam claims she was exposed to toxic chemicals at a chemical plant. The exposure occurred in 2007, but she did not experience any symptoms until 2020, and only discovered the cause of her illness in 2021. She commences an action in 2023. The defendant argues the action is barred by the ultimate limitation period, even if not by the basic two-year period.
call_of_question: Under s. 15 of the Limitations Act, 2002, what is the ultimate limitation period, from what date does it run, and can it extinguish Miriam's claim?

options.A: The ultimate limitation period is 10 years from the date the claim was discovered; Miriam's claim was discovered in 2021, so the ultimate period will not expire until 2031.
options.B: The ultimate limitation period is 15 years from the date the act or omission occurred. Since the exposure occurred in 2007, the ultimate period expired in 2022, barring Miriam's 2023 claim regardless of when she discovered it.
options.C: There is no ultimate limitation period in Ontario; the two-year basic period is the only limitation that applies.
options.D: The ultimate limitation period is 15 years from the date of discovery; since Miriam discovered her claim in 2021, the ultimate period will not expire until 2036.

explanation: Under s. 15(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, no proceeding shall be commenced in respect of a claim after the 15th anniversary of the day on which the act or omission on which the claim is based took place. This ultimate period runs from the date of the act or omission — not from the date of discovery — and operates as an absolute bar regardless of when the claim was discovered. The chemical exposure in 2007 triggers the 15-year clock; it expires in 2022. Miriam's 2023 action is therefore barred by s. 15, even though she only discovered her claim in 2021 and the two-year basic period would otherwise have permitted her to sue until 2023.
why_A_wrong: The ultimate limitation period is 15 years, not 10 years. It also runs from the date of the act or omission, not from the date of discovery.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 15(1) provides a 15-year ultimate limitation period running from the date of the act or omission; the exposure in 2007 means the period expired in 2022, barring Miriam's 2023 action.
why_C_wrong: Ontario's Limitations Act, 2002 does establish an ultimate limitation period in s. 15. It is a fundamental feature of the Act, distinct from the basic two-year period.
why_D_wrong: The 15-year ultimate period does not run from the date of discovery. It runs from the date of the act or omission under s. 15(1), regardless of when the claim was discovered.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: The Ultimate 15-Year Limitation Period
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 15(1)

tested_concepts: runs from date of act — not discovery

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Suspension of Limitation Period — Minors and Persons Under Disability
fact_pattern: Theo, age 6, is injured in a car accident on March 10, 2022. His parents do not commence an action on his behalf. On his 18th birthday in 2034, Theo learns that the limitation period for his claim may have expired.
call_of_question: Under s. 6 of the Limitations Act, 2002, how does Theo's status as a minor affect the running of the two-year limitation period?

options.A: The two-year limitation period began running on March 10, 2022, the date of the accident; Theo's minority has no effect on the limitation period.
options.B: The limitation period is suspended while Theo is a minor. It begins to run on the day he ceases to be a minor (his 18th birthday), so Theo has until his 20th birthday to commence an action.
options.C: The limitation period is extended to 10 years for minors; Theo has until March 10, 2032 to commence an action.
options.D: There is no limitation period for claims by minors injured in motor vehicle accidents.

explanation: Under s. 6(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the limitation period established by s. 4 does not run during any period in which the person with the claim is a minor. Section 6(2) further provides that, for the purposes of the basic two-year limitation period in s. 4, a minor's claim is discovered on the day the minor ceases to be a minor or, if earlier, on the day a litigation guardian is appointed for the minor. Theo turns 18 in 2034; his two-year limitation period begins running on his 18th birthday and expires on his 20th birthday in 2036. Minority suspends — does not eliminate — the limitation period.
why_A_wrong: Section 6 of the Limitations Act, 2002 expressly suspends the running of the limitation period during minority. The two-year period does not run against a minor.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 6, the limitation period is suspended during minority and begins to run on the day Theo turns 18; he has two years from that date.
why_C_wrong: There is no automatic 10-year limitation period for minors. The basic two-year period applies but does not start running until the minor attains the age of majority.
why_D_wrong: The suspension under s. 6 applies to motor vehicle accident claims but does not eliminate the limitation period entirely — it merely defers the start of the two-year period until the claimant turns 18.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Suspension of Limitation Period — Minors and Persons Under Disability
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, ss. 6(1), 6(2)

tested_concepts: s. 6 suspension of limitation period for minors
minority tolls but does not eliminate limitation

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Limitation Period for Contribution and Indemnity Claims
fact_pattern: Yolanda is sued by a plaintiff in July 2024 for negligence. Yolanda believes that a third party, Conrad, contributed to the plaintiff's loss and wishes to claim contribution and indemnity from Conrad by issuing a third-party claim. The plaintiff's accident occurred in June 2022.
call_of_question: Under s. 18 of the Limitations Act, 2002, when does the limitation period for Yolanda's contribution and indemnity claim against Conrad begin to run?

options.A: June 2022, when the accident giving rise to the plaintiff's loss occurred
options.B: The date Yolanda first knew or ought to have known that she had a claim against Conrad for contribution or indemnity — which typically arises when a claim is made against Yolanda in the main action
options.C: June 2024, two years after the accident, which is the earliest that a contribution claim can be made
options.D: There is no limitation period for contribution and indemnity claims because they are derivative of the main action

explanation: Under s. 18 of the Limitations Act, 2002, a claim for contribution and indemnity is discovered on the day on which the person making the claim first knew or ought to have known that they had the right to make the claim. For contribution claims, this typically means the day on which a claim is made against the party seeking contribution — because it is only at that point that the party knows it may have suffered a loss giving rise to a right of contribution. Yolanda's two-year limitation period for her contribution claim against Conrad therefore began to run when she was served with the plaintiff's claim in July 2024, not on the date of the accident in June 2022. She has until approximately July 2026 to issue the third-party claim.
why_A_wrong: The limitation period for a contribution claim does not run from the date of the underlying accident. Section 18 ties discovery of the contribution claim to when the claimant knew or ought to have known of their right to claim contribution — typically when sued in the main action.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s. 18, Yolanda's contribution claim was discovered when she was named as a defendant in July 2024; her two-year period runs from that date.
why_C_wrong: There is no rule that contribution claims may only be made two years after the underlying accident. The limitation runs from discovery under s. 18.
why_D_wrong: Contribution and indemnity claims are subject to limitation periods under s. 18 of the Limitations Act, 2002. They are not exempt merely because they are derivative of a main action.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period for Contribution and Indemnity Claims
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 18

tested_concepts: s. 18 contribution and indemnity limitation period
discovery runs from when named as defendant

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: Yusuf lent $40,000 to his friend Andrei under a written loan agreement in 2015. The agreement stated that the loan was repayable 'on demand.' Yusuf never made a demand for repayment. In March 2025, Yusuf makes a demand and then commences an action one month later when Andrei refuses to pay.
call_of_question: Under the Limitations Act, 2002, when does the two-year limitation period begin to run for a demand obligation, and is Yusuf's claim statute-barred?

options.A: The two-year period began in 2015 when the loan was made; Yusuf's 2025 action is statute-barred.
options.B: The two-year period begins when Yusuf makes a demand; since Yusuf made his demand in March 2025 and commenced within two years of that demand, his claim is not statute-barred.
options.C: There is no limitation period for demand obligations; Yusuf may sue at any time as long as the debt has not been paid.
options.D: The two-year period begins when Yusuf first learned Andrei was unable to pay; since Yusuf did not know Andrei was financially troubled before 2025, his claim is not statute-barred.

explanation: Under s. 5(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002 (as interpreted by the courts), for a demand obligation — a debt payable on demand — the limitation period runs from the date on which the demand is made, because the cause of action does not arise until demand is made and refused (or until a reasonable time for compliance has passed). However, s. 5(1) and the surrounding case law also impose an objective standard: the obligation is deemed to have been discovered no later than it would have been discovered with reasonable diligence. For a demand obligation, discovery occurs when demand is made. Since Yusuf made his demand in March 2025 and commenced his action within two months, his claim is not statute-barred — provided the ultimate 15-year limitation period under s. 15 does not also apply (which it would not on these facts, as the loan was in 2015 and 15 years have not yet passed).
why_A_wrong: For demand obligations, the cause of action and therefore the limitation period do not begin running at the time of the loan or the original agreement — they begin when demand is made. Yusuf's failure to demand repayment earlier did not cause the limitation period to run before demand was made.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. For a demand obligation, the limitation period begins when demand is made; Yusuf's March 2025 demand starts the two-year clock, and commencing within one month is well within time.
why_C_wrong: Demand obligations are subject to limitation periods under the Limitations Act, 2002. The period begins when demand is made; there is no unlimited right to sue indefinitely.
why_D_wrong: The limitation period for a demand obligation does not depend on when the creditor learns of the debtor's financial difficulties. Discovery occurs when demand is made; Andrei's financial status before the demand is not the triggering event.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Limitation Period for Demand Obligations
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s. 5

tested_concepts: demand obligation — limitation begins when demand made

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Limitations Act Exceptions — No-Limitation Proceedings Under s.16
fact_pattern: A prospective client contacts Leblanc & Associates LLP to discuss a claim to enforce an undisputed court judgment obtained 17 years ago. The client asks whether the limitation period has expired for enforcing the judgment, given that neither the basic two-year period nor the 15-year ultimate period appears to have been met.
call_of_question: Under s.16 of the Limitations Act, 2002, are there any proceedings to which no limitation period applies?

options.A: No — every civil proceeding in Ontario is subject to either the two-year basic period or the 15-year ultimate limitation period; no exceptions exist.
options.B: Yes — under s.16 of the Limitations Act, 2002, certain proceedings have no limitation period, including proceedings to enforce court orders and proceedings by the Crown or a public authority for recovery of possession of public land.
options.C: Yes — proceedings to enforce a court judgment are subject to a 30-year limitation period, not the standard two-year or 15-year periods.
options.D: No — court judgments are subject to the basic two-year limitation period running from the date the judgment was granted.

explanation: Under s.16 of the Limitations Act, 2002 (S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B), there is no limitation period in respect of certain proceedings, including: a proceeding to enforce a court order; a proceeding to enforce an order for support or maintenance; and a proceeding by the Crown in right of Ontario to recover possession of public land. Because there is no limitation period for proceedings to enforce a court order under s.16, the client's claim to enforce the 17-year-old judgment is not statute-barred — no limitation period applies to such a proceeding.
why_A_wrong: Section 16 of the Limitations Act, 2002 expressly exempts certain proceedings from any limitation period. Proceedings to enforce court orders are among those specifically exempted.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 16 provides that no limitation period applies to proceedings to enforce court orders, among other enumerated proceedings.
why_C_wrong: There is no 30-year limitation period in the Limitations Act, 2002 for enforcing court judgments. Section 16 provides that no limitation period applies to such proceedings; there is no specific extended period.
why_D_wrong: The basic two-year limitation period does not apply to enforcement of court orders. Section 16 expressly exempts proceedings to enforce a court order from the Act's limitation periods.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Limitations Act Exceptions — No-Limitation Proceedings Under s.16
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.16

tested_concepts: s.16 — no limitation period for enforcement of court orders
exceptions to the basic and ultimate limitation periods

subject: civil_litigation
fact_pattern: A commercial contract between Nakamura Consulting Inc. and Petrov Industries Ltd. contains a clause stating: 'Any claim arising from this agreement must be commenced within six months of the event giving rise to the claim, failing which all claims are forever barred.' Two years after a breach occurs, Nakamura wishes to sue Petrov but is confronted with this contractual limitation clause.
call_of_question: Under the Limitations Act, 2002, is a contractual clause shortening the limitation period to six months enforceable between commercial parties?

options.A: Yes — parties to a commercial contract may freely agree to any limitation period shorter or longer than the statutory period; such agreements are always enforceable.
options.B: Yes — under s.22 of the Limitations Act, 2002, parties to a business agreement may agree to vary or exclude the limitation periods in the Act; a six-month contractual limitation period between commercial parties is therefore enforceable.
options.C: No — the two-year limitation period in s.4 of the Limitations Act, 2002 is mandatory and cannot be shortened by contract; any clause purporting to shorten it is void.
options.D: No — contractual limitation periods require court approval to be enforceable; the parties cannot unilaterally agree to alter the statutory limitation period.

explanation: Under s.22 of the Limitations Act, 2002 (S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B), parties to a business agreement — defined as an agreement made in the course of a business between persons each of whom is acting in a business capacity — may agree to vary or exclude the limitation periods provided for by the Act. Commercial parties dealing at arm's length in a business context may therefore validly agree to a shorter limitation period (such as six months) for claims arising from their agreement. The result is that Nakamura's claim may be barred by the contractual limitation clause, even though the statutory period has not expired. Consumer agreements are treated differently — the Act restricts the ability to limit consumer rights.
why_A_wrong: The ability to vary limitation periods is not unlimited for all types of parties. The Act distinguishes between business agreements (where variation is permitted under s.22) and consumer agreements (where variation is more restricted). However, between commercial parties, variation is permissible.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 22 of the Limitations Act, 2002 permits commercial parties to vary limitation periods by agreement; a six-month contractual period in a business agreement is enforceable.
why_C_wrong: The two-year limitation period is not mandatory and unalterable between commercial parties. Section 22 of the Act expressly permits business parties to vary the statutory periods by agreement.
why_D_wrong: No court approval is required for parties to agree to vary limitation periods under s.22. The Act permits such agreements without any judicial oversight requirement.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Agreement to Vary Limitation Periods
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.22

tested_concepts: s.22 — business parties may vary limitation periods
contractual shorter limitation period enforceable in business agreement
distinction between business and consumer agreements

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Suspension of Limitation Period — Persons with Psychological Incapacity
fact_pattern: Rosamund experienced severe psychological trauma in 2019 as a result of an assault. Her psychological condition rendered her unable to appreciate the nature of her legal rights or manage her legal affairs from the date of the assault through June 2023, when she was treated and regained capacity. She commences an action against her assailant in May 2025.
call_of_question: Under s.7 of the Limitations Act, 2002, how does Rosamund's psychological incapacity affect the running of the two-year limitation period, and is her claim timely?

options.A: Psychological incapacity has no effect on the running of a limitation period; the two-year period ran from 2019 and Rosamund's claim is statute-barred.
options.B: Under s.7, the limitation period is suspended during any period in which the claimant is a person with incapacity who is not represented by a litigation guardian; Rosamund's period was suspended from 2019 to June 2023, began running in June 2023, and expires in June 2025 — making her May 2025 claim likely statute-barred.
options.C: Under s.7, the limitation period is suspended during a period of incapacity, so Rosamund's two-year period would not begin until June 2023; commencing in May 2025 is within two years of regaining capacity.
options.D: Rosamund's assault claim has no limitation period because it arises from a criminal act; all claims for damages from criminal assault are exempt from the Limitations Act, 2002.

explanation: Under s.7(1) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the limitation period established by s.4 does not run during any period in which the person with the claim is incapable of commencing a proceeding because of psychological or physical incapacity and is not represented by a litigation guardian. 'Incapacity' for this purpose means the person is not reasonably able to understand the nature of the claim or to determine whether it is appropriate to commence a proceeding. During the period from 2019 to June 2023, Rosamund was incapable of appreciating her legal rights due to her psychological condition. The two-year period was suspended for that entire period and began running in June 2023 when she regained capacity. Her May 2025 action is within two years of June 2023 and is timely.
why_A_wrong: Section 7 of the Limitations Act, 2002 expressly provides that the limitation period does not run during a period of incapacity. Psychological incapacity is a recognized ground for suspension under s.7.
why_B_wrong: Option B incorrectly states that the two-year period 'expires in June 2025 — making her May 2025 claim likely statute-barred.' While the expiry math in B is arithmetically accurate, the premise of option B — that s.7 suspends during incapacity and the period runs from June 2023 — is the same as option C. Option C is superior because it correctly concludes that commencing in May 2025 'is within two years of regaining capacity' (the fact pattern states she regained capacity in June 2023; May 2025 is within two years of that). Option B's conclusion (claim likely barred) is incorrect based on the fact pattern.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under s.7, the limitation period is suspended during psychological incapacity; Rosamund's two-year period began running in June 2023 when she regained capacity, making a May 2025 commencement within two years and therefore timely.
why_D_wrong: There is no blanket exemption from the Limitations Act, 2002 for claims arising from criminal assaults. Such claims are civil proceedings subject to the Act's limitation periods, though the s.7 suspension for incapacity may apply as it does here.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Suspension of Limitation Period — Persons with Psychological Incapacity
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.7

tested_concepts: s.7 — limitation period suspended during incapacity

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Ultimate Limitation Period — Exceptions Under s.15(4)
fact_pattern: Szymanski was sexually assaulted by her former employer when she was 15 years old. The assault occurred in 2000. Szymanski is now 38 and wishes to bring a civil claim for damages from the sexual assault. The defendant argues that the 15-year ultimate limitation period under s.15 of the Limitations Act, 2002 expired in 2015, barring her claim absolutely.
call_of_question: Under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002, does the 15-year ultimate limitation period apply to a civil claim for damages arising from sexual assault?

options.A: Yes — the 15-year ultimate limitation period is absolute and applies to all civil claims in Ontario without exception.
options.B: No — under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002, the 15-year ultimate limitation period does not apply to proceedings based on claims for sexual assault; there is no ultimate period for such claims.
options.C: Yes — but the 15-year period is suspended while the claimant was a minor, so it began running on Szymanski's 18th birthday in 2003 and expires in 2018; her claim is therefore barred.
options.D: No — the 15-year ultimate period does not apply to any personal injury claim, including Szymanski's.

explanation: Under s.15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002 (S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B), the 15-year ultimate limitation period in s.15(1) does not apply to a claim for sexual assault. The Legislature recognizes that survivors of sexual assault often require many years before they are psychologically able to bring a civil claim, and that an absolute ultimate limitation period would be unjust in such circumstances. Accordingly, s.15(4) creates an express exception to the ultimate limitation period for sexual assault claims. Szymanski's claim is not barred by the 15-year ultimate period. Under s.16(1)(h) of the Limitations Act, 2002, no limitation period applies to a claim arising from sexual assault — neither the basic two-year period under s.4 nor the ultimate 15-year period under s.15 applies to Szymanski's claim. Her civil claim for damages from the sexual assault is not subject to any limitation period.
why_A_wrong: The 15-year ultimate limitation period is not absolute and without exception. Section 15(4) of the Limitations Act, 2002 expressly excludes sexual assault claims from the operation of the ultimate period.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 15(4) provides that the 15-year ultimate limitation period does not apply to claims for sexual assault, reflecting the Legislature's recognition of the particular circumstances of sexual assault survivors.
why_C_wrong: The ultimate limitation period does not apply to sexual assault claims under s.15(4), so arguments about suspending it during minority are irrelevant. More fundamentally, s.15(4) removes the sexual assault claim from the scope of s.15 entirely.
why_D_wrong: The s.15(4) exception is specific to sexual assault, not to all personal injury claims. Other personal injury claims remain subject to the 15-year ultimate limitation period under s.15(1). The exception is narrow and specific.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Ultimate Limitation Period — Exceptions Under s.15(4)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Limitations Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 24, Sched. B, s.15(4)

tested_concepts: s.15(4) — sexual assault claims exempt from 15-year ultimate period
ultimate limitation period not absolute — has exceptions
contrast with general personal injury claims
